summary:
## This month in numbers
Australia experienced its 4th warmest February–April on record, with an average temperature of 26.32°C, a significant +1.3°C above the 1961–1990 baseline. Globally, land temperatures for April 2026 ranked as the 2nd warmest on record, at 14.96°C, which is +1.1°C above the baseline. The February–April 2026 global land temperature also ranked 2nd warmest, at 14.13°C, an anomaly of +1.2°C.

## What changed
While April 2026 saw Australia's average temperature at 22.61°C, only +0.3°C above the baseline, the preceding months of February and March were considerably warmer, with anomalies of +1.7°C and +2.0°C respectively. This contributed to Australia's 4th warmest February–April period on record. New South Wales experienced its second driest April on record, receiving only 13% of its average rainfall for the month. This dryness has led to increased rainfall deficiencies in north-eastern New South Wales, bordering Queensland, and in parts of Tasmania. Australia's 1-month anomaly for April placed it 224th out of 234 regions globally, indicating a relatively cooler month compared to many other parts of the world.

## What’s driving change?
The current climate patterns are heavily influenced by the developing El Niño. Although the El Niño–Southern Oscillation (ENSO) is currently in a neutral phase, a rapid warming phase in the Pacific Ocean began in May, with an 82% probability of El Niño emerging between May and July 2026, and a 96% chance it will persist through the end of the year. El Niño typically brings warmer and drier conditions to eastern and northern Australia, increasing the risk of drought and heatwaves. This is already evident with 36 wildfire events recorded in Australia from May 4th to May 19th, representing 100% of the annual total, an unusual concentration for the period. Additionally, a drought event has been active since late February. The Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) is currently neutral, but a positive IOD, which restricts moisture flow to southern Australia, remains possible in the coming winter and spring and could intensify dryness if it coincides with El Niño.

## Looking ahead
The Bureau of Meteorology's long-range forecast for May to July 2026 indicates below-average rainfall across much of eastern and south-western Australia, with above-average maximum temperatures expected across most of the continent, particularly in Victoria, Tasmania, eastern New South Wales, and southern Queensland.
